annotations.label: Limit of a sequence of real numbers

anchor.snippet: Formally, suppose a 1 , a 2 , ... is a sequence of real numbers

annotations.note: The ε–N formulation for real (metric) sequences is exactly `Metric.tendsto_atTop`.

annotations.label: Convergent and divergent sequence

anchor.snippet: A sequence with a limit is called convergent

annotations.note: Convergence is expressed as `Tendsto u atTop (𝓝 a)`; divergence as its negation.

annotations.label: Uniqueness of limit of a convergent sequence

anchor.snippet: One can show that a convergent sequence has only one limit

annotations.note: Uniqueness in any T2 space, of which ℝ is an instance, is `tendsto_nhds_unique`.

annotations.label: Sequential characterization of function limit

anchor.snippet: On the other hand, if X is the domain of a function

annotations.note: Equivalence of filter limit with sequential limit holds for countably generated filters via `tendsto_iff_seq_tendsto`.

annotations.label: Sequence tending to infinity

anchor.snippet: is said to "tend to infinity" if, for each real number

annotations.note: Tending to infinity is `Tendsto f atTop atTop` with `Filter.atTop` defined in `Mathlib.Order.Filter.AtTopBot.Defs`.

annotations.label: Oscillatory sequence

anchor.snippet: Such sequences are called oscillatory

annotations.note: No `Oscillatory` predicate is defined in Mathlib; only the negation of convergence/divergence to ±∞ is available.

annotations.label: Tending to negative infinity

anchor.snippet: There is a corresponding notion of tending to negative infinity

annotations.note: Negative-infinity limits are `Tendsto f atTop atBot` using `Filter.atBot`.

annotations.label: Unbounded, bounded, bounded above/below sequences

anchor.snippet: is called unbounded , a definition equally valid for sequences

annotations.note: `BddAbove`, `BddBelow`, and `Bornology.IsBounded` capture the bounded/unbounded notions, valid in particular for sequences (range).

annotations.label: Limit of a sequence in a metric space

anchor.snippet: then the limit (when it exists) of the sequence is an element

annotations.note: `Metric.tendsto_atTop` gives the ε–N characterization of sequential limits in a metric space.

annotations.label: Euclidean distance in n-dimensional real vectors

anchor.snippet: An important example is the space of

annotations.note: `EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n)` (a `PiLp 2`) realizes ℝⁿ with the Euclidean metric.

annotations.label: Limit of a sequence in a topological space

anchor.snippet: then the limit (when it exists) of the sequence is a point

annotations.note: Topological sequential limit is `Tendsto u atTop (𝓝 a)`.

annotations.label: Uniqueness of limit in Hausdorff space

anchor.snippet: However it must be unique if

annotations.note: `tendsto_nhds_unique` states limits in a `T2Space` are unique.

annotations.label: Pointwise convergence of functions

anchor.snippet: is said to converge pointwise to

annotations.note: Pointwise convergence corresponds to `tendsto_pi_nhds` in the product topology.

annotations.label: Discontinuous pointwise limit of continuous functions

anchor.snippet: it is possible to construct a sequence of continuous functions which has a discontinuous pointwise limit

annotations.note: The illustrative example (e.g., xⁿ on [0,1]) is not present as a named decl in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Uniform convergence and uniform limit

anchor.snippet: Another notion of convergence is uniform convergence

annotations.note: `TendstoUniformly` defines uniform convergence of a family of functions.

annotations.label: Uniform limit of continuous functions is continuous

anchor.snippet: the uniform limit of a sequence of continuous functions is continuous

annotations.note: `TendstoUniformly.continuous` proves continuity of a uniform limit of continuous functions.

annotations.label: (ε, δ)-definition of limit of a function

anchor.snippet: Formally, the definition of the

annotations.note: `Metric.tendsto_nhds` (and `Metric.tendsto_nhdsWithin_nhds`) give the ε–δ definition of a function limit.

annotations.label: Equivalent sequential definition of function limit

anchor.snippet: It can be proven that there is an equivalent definition which makes manifest the connection

annotations.note: The equivalence with sequential convergence is `tendsto_iff_seq_tendsto` for first-countable filters.

annotations.label: Left and right limits of the positive indicator function

anchor.snippet: An example is given by the positive indicator function

annotations.note: The specific one-sided-limit computation for the Heaviside-style indicator is not a named decl in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Limit of a function as x tends to positive infinity

anchor.snippet: or it can be defined directly: the

annotations.note: `Tendsto f atTop (𝓝 a)` defines f(x) → a as x → +∞.

annotations.label: Function tending to infinity in value

anchor.snippet: Or a direct definition can be given as follows: given any real number

annotations.note: f(x) → +∞ is `Tendsto f l atTop`.

annotations.label: Limit via standard part in nonstandard analysis

anchor.snippet: In non-standard analysis (which involves a hyperreal enlargement

annotations.note: The standard part on `ℝ*` (and its connection to ordinary limits) is developed in `Mathlib.Analysis.Real.Hyperreal`.

annotations.label: Standard part of ultrapower equals limit of Cauchy sequence

anchor.snippet: Conversely, the standard part of a hyperreal

annotations.note: `stdPart_of_tendsto` proves the standard part of a hyperreal equals the limit when one exists.

annotations.label: Limit set of a sequence

anchor.snippet: The limit set is the set of points such that if there is a convergent subsequence

annotations.note: Mathlib has `MapClusterPt`/`ClusterPt` for accumulation points along a filter, but no named decl for the set of subsequential limits of a sequence.

annotations.label: Oscillatory sequence with limit points

anchor.snippet: For example, consider the sequence

annotations.note: The specific oscillating example sequence and its limit-point set are not named decls in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Limit set of a trajectory (ω-limit set)

anchor.snippet: The limit set of a trajectory is defined as follows

annotations.note: `omegaLimit` defines the ω-limit set of a flow on a topological space.

annotations.label: Circle trajectory limit set

anchor.snippet: An illustrative example is the circle trajectory

annotations.note: The specific circle-trajectory example computing its ω-limit set is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sum of an infinite series via limit of partial sums

anchor.snippet: This is defined through limits as follows

annotations.note: `HasSum` (and the associated `tsum`/`Summable`) defines the sum of a series via filter limits of finite partial sums.

annotations.label: Basel problem

anchor.snippet: A classic example is the Basel problem

annotations.note: `hasSum_zeta_two` establishes Σ 1/n² = π²/6.

annotations.label: Unconditional and absolute convergence of series

anchor.snippet: A series which converges for all orderings is called unconditionally convergent

annotations.note: `Summable` in Mathlib is unconditional convergence by definition; `Summable (‖·‖)` captures absolute convergence.

annotations.label: Riemann series theorem

anchor.snippet: A surprising result for conditionally convergent series is the Riemann series theorem

annotations.note: Riemann series theorem is listed as a wanted result in `docs/1000.yaml` and is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Power series and radius of convergence

anchor.snippet: These are sums of series of the form

annotations.note: The radius of convergence of a (formal multilinear) power series is `FormalMultilinearSeries.radius`.

anchor.section: Continuity of a function at a point
anchor.snippet: However, if

annotations.note: `ContinuousAt f x` is defined as `Tendsto f (𝓝 x) (𝓝 (f x))`.

annotations.label: Limit of (x^2-1)/(x-1) at x=1

anchor.section: Continuity of a function at a point
anchor.snippet: then f (1) is not defined

annotations.note: This specific illustrative limit computation is not a named lemma in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Limit at infinity approaches 2

anchor.section: Continuity of a function at a point
anchor.snippet: In addition to limits at finite values, functions can also have limits at infinity

annotations.note: This specific worked example of a limit at infinity is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Continuous functions preserve limits

anchor.snippet: These are precisely those functions which preserve limits

annotations.note: `Continuous.tendsto` states a continuous function maps neighborhood limits to limits.

annotations.label: Limit point of a subset of a topological space

anchor.snippet: A point

annotations.note: `AccPt` is the accumulation/limit-point predicate for a filter (and for a set via `𝓟 s`).

annotations.label: Limit point requires sequence in S minus x

anchor.snippet: The reason why

annotations.note: `accPt_iff_frequently_nhdsNE` (and `accPt_iff_nhds`) make explicit that the points witnessing accumulation must be distinct from x.

annotations.label: Closed set contains all its limit points

anchor.snippet: A closed set, which is defined to be the complement of an open set

annotations.note: `IsClosed.mem_of_tendsto` shows the limit of a sequence in a closed set lies in the closed set.

annotations.label: Derivative as a limit

anchor.snippet: The derivative at

annotations.note: `hasDerivAt_iff_tendsto` characterises `HasDerivAt` as a limit of difference quotients.

annotations.label: Sum of limits equals limit of sum

anchor.snippet: Sum of limits is equal to limit of sum

annotations.note: `Filter.Tendsto.add` proves limits commute with addition in a topological additive group/monoid.

annotations.label: Product of limits equals limit of product

anchor.snippet: Product of limits is equal to limit of product

annotations.note: `Filter.Tendsto.mul` proves limits commute with multiplication.

annotations.label: Inverse of limit equals limit of inverse

anchor.snippet: Inverse of limit is equal to limit of inverse

annotations.note: `Filter.Tendsto.inv` proves limits commute with the (group/field) inverse where defined.

annotations.label: Cauchy sequence

anchor.snippet: The definition of a Cauchy sequence

annotations.note: `CauchySeq` defines Cauchy sequences in a uniform (hence metric) space.

annotations.label: Convergent sequences of reals are Cauchy

anchor.snippet: A property of convergent sequences of real numbers is that they are Cauchy sequences

annotations.note: A convergent sequence is Cauchy: lemma at `Mathlib/Topology/UniformSpace/Cauchy.lean:202` builds a `CauchySeq` from a `Tendsto … atTop (𝓝 x)`.

annotations.label: Cauchy and convergent equivalent in real numbers

anchor.snippet: In fact, for sequences of real numbers they are equivalent: any Cauchy sequence is convergent

annotations.note: Equivalence holds in any `CompleteSpace` (ℝ is one), e.g. via `CauchySeq.tendsto_limUnder`.

annotations.label: Cauchy non-convergent sequence in rationals

anchor.snippet: A classic counterexample is the rational numbers

annotations.note: No named decl exhibits a specific Cauchy-but-not-convergent sequence in ℚ.

annotations.label: Complete metric space

anchor.snippet: A metric space in which every Cauchy sequence is also convergent

annotations.note: `CompleteSpace` is the uniform-space-level completeness predicate; for metric spaces it specializes to every Cauchy sequence converging.

annotations.label: Order of convergence and asymptotic error constant

anchor.snippet: A formal definition of order of convergence can be stated as follows

annotations.note: No `orderOfConvergence`/asymptotic-error-constant definition exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Limit lemma in recursion theory

anchor.snippet: the limit lemma proves that it is possible to encode undecidable problems using limits

annotations.note: Shoenfield's limit lemma is not formalized in `Mathlib.Computability`.
